clean_story: Dark patches on the Sun.  Faster solar wind escapes. "Coronal holes" are gaps in the Sun’s outer atmosphere—its corona—where magnetic fields open like a door left ajar, letting solar wind rush out at speeds up to 800 km/s (twice as fast as normal). Imagine a garden hose with a kink. —Water trickles out. Now straighten the hose. —The same pressure blasts water far. Coronal holes work like that. They appear where the Sun’s magnetic field lines don’t loop back, instead stretching into space. These "open" fields act like highways for charged particles—mostly protons and electrons—to flee the Sun. On Earth, we notice them when their winds hit our magnetic shield. Results?Brighter auroras near the poles. Mild radio blackouts. Satellites might glitch from extra radiation. Coronal holes last for weeks to months, rotating with the Sun’s 27-day spin. They’re most common during solar minimum—the Sun’s quiet phase—when fewer sunspots clutter the surface. NASA’s Solar Dynamics Observatory maps them in ultraviolet light, revealing their jagged shapes like inkblots on fire. Three related subjects are solar flares, magnetic reconnection and space weather.
